title: Chapter 5: The Hound's Last Hunt
content: Baron Vereck arrived seven minutes after sunrise.

The door didn't just open — it *exploded*. Wood splintered, iron hinges shrieked, and the reinforced frame buckled inward as two hundred pounds of enraged vampire crashed through it in a blur of shadow and claws. Vereck had been tracking Kael for three days. He'd smelled the fledgling's blood on the wind, followed the trail of salt and garlic and Silver Dawn gear, and decided — correctly — that his prey was hiding in human territory.
What he hadn't expected was the garlic.

The crushed cloves hit him first. His eyes, enhanced for night hunting, flooded with tears so fast he was temporarily blind. The garlic water on the windowsills vaporized on contact with his skin, releasing a cloud of burning mist that seared his throat and lungs. He stumbled, one hand going to his face, and stepped directly onto the ankle stakes.
The wood went through his boot like it was paper.

Vereck screamed — a raw, animal sound that had nothing to do with the cold arrogance he'd projected in the Ruins. Vampire blood, dark and thick as oil, splattered across the apartment floor. He didn't fall — Barons were tougher than that — but he staggered, his weight coming down wrong on his wounded foot, and Kael saw the moment the threshold rule activated.
Vereck's power plummeted.

It was visible. The Baron's shadow-step aura — that dark shimmer that let him dissolve and reform — flickered and died. His speed, which should have been ten times Kael's, dropped to maybe twice. His strength, which should have been overwhelming, became merely dangerous. The uninvited threshold was sapping him moment by moment, and the garlic was making him blind, and the stakes were making him bleed, and he still hadn't even *found* Kael in the room.
"WHERE ARE YOU, YOU LITTLE—"
"Here."
Kael stepped out of the sunlight.

Vereck's head snapped toward the sound. His tears were clearing, his vision returning in blurry fragments. He saw a figure. A fledgling. Pale, thin, still shaking slightly from the hunger he hadn't fully learned to control. Easy prey. Even at half power, easy prey.
Then his gaze moved past Kael to the window. To the sunlight. To the impossible sight of a vampire standing in broad daylight without so much as a wisp of smoke rising from his skin.
"What—" Vereck's voice cracked. "What *are* you?"

"Someone who learned the rules before fighting you." Kael picked up the silver crossbow from the shelf. "You've hunted twelve unclaimed fledglings. You called them lambs. You told them the same thing you told me — that they couldn't run, couldn't hide, couldn't fight back."
He raised the crossbow.
"I'm not a lamb."
The bolt caught Vereck in the shoulder — not the heart, because Kael hadn't been aiming for the heart. A wounded Baron was a slow Baron. A slow Baron was a dead Baron.
Vereck lunged.

Even at half power, he was fast. His claws raked across Kael's ribs, carving four parallel lines of fire that soaked his shirt with blood before he even felt the pain. The Pact answered immediately — Sera's light flooding through the bond, knitting flesh, dulling the agony. Kael stumbled but didn't fall. He'd been a soldier. He'd been shot before. Pain was just information.
He drove a stake into Vereck's thigh and twisted.
The scream that followed was almost satisfying.
The fight lasted ninety seconds. It felt like ninety years.

Vereck was stronger, faster, more experienced. Every one of his strikes was deadly — a throat-rip, a heart-punch, a skull-crush — and Kael survived each one by inches. The garlic was his shield. The stakes were his claws. The threshold rule was his armor, cutting Vereck's power in half and then half again as the apartment itself rejected his presence.
But what Kael had that Vereck didn't was the sun.

He maneuvered the fight toward the window. Every dodge, every counter-strike, every desperate roll across the garlic-scattered floor worked to force Vereck into the light. The Baron knew what he was doing — he fought to stay in the shadows, to keep the sunlight at his back — but he was wounded and blind and losing strength with every passing second.
Kael grabbed him by the throat.

It was a reckless move. Vereck's claws came up immediately, sinking into Kael's forearm, tearing muscle and tendon. But Kael didn't let go. He locked his grip and *pushed*, driving the Baron backward toward the window, toward the light, toward the one thing even a Baron couldn't survive.
Vereck's eyes went wide. "No— please— I'll serve you, I'll do anything—"
"You called me a lamb," Kael said. "Remember?"
He thrust Vereck into the full morning sun.

The Baron didn't burn immediately. He *melted*. Flesh sloughed from bone like wax from a candle. Smoke poured from his screaming mouth in thick black clouds. His eyes boiled in their sockets, the enhanced vision that had tracked a hundred fledglings across miles of wasteland evaporating in seconds. His claws, still embedded in Kael's arm, crumbled to ash.
When it was over, there was no body. Just a pile of grey dust on the apartment floor and a single dark, pulsing object where Vereck's heart had been.
The heart-blood.

Kael knelt. His arm was still bleeding despite the Pact's healing — Vereck's claws had gone deep, and the venom on them was Baron-strength. But the pain was distant now, replaced by something else. Anticipation. Hunger. The thing in front of him wasn't just an organ. It was power. Rank. The accumulated strength of every vampire Vereck had ever killed, compressed into a jewel of dark blood.
He picked it up. It was warm. Still beating, somehow, with a rhythm that wasn't quite a heartbeat.
"Kael—" Sera's voice came through the Pact. She'd felt the fight, felt his wounds, felt the moment Vereck's life had ended. "Are you—"
"I'm fine." He wasn't fine. He was bleeding and exhausted and his hands were shaking. But he was alive, and Vereck wasn't, and the heart-blood was pulsing in his palm like a promise.
He ate.
The power hit him in three waves.
First: pain. Every bone in his body shifted. His fangs lengthened. His muscles thickened. The wounds on his arm and ribs sealed over in seconds, the scar tissue forming and fading in the same breath.

Second: expansion. His senses exploded outward. He could hear the old woman's heartbeat in the bedroom — fast, terrified, but alive. He could hear Sera's heartbeat through the Pact — steady, relieved, threaded with golden light. He could feel the boundaries of Vereck's territory pressing against his awareness, a domain that was now unclaimed, now available, now *his* if he wanted it.
Third: understanding. He was no longer a fledgling. He was a Baron.

He stood. The sunlight, which had been warm before, was now almost hot — not painfully, but noticeably. His tolerance was still absolute, but his senses were sharper. He could feel the individual photons hitting his skin, a gentle percussion of light that most vampires would have experienced as agony.

The old woman opened her bedroom door. She looked at the pile of ash on her floor, at the bloody stakes scattered across her apartment, at the vampire standing in her sunlit living room with blood on his hands and something new burning in his eyes.
"You killed him," she said.
"Yes."
"Good." She crossed herself, the gesture automatic, a lifetime of faith responding to the impossible. "What happens now?"

Kael looked out the window. The sun was fully up, painting the Ruins in shades of gold and shadow. Somewhere beneath those ruins, a city of vampires slept through the day, unaware that the rules had just changed.
"Now," he said, "I find a bigger target."
